skill.name: waterfall-development
skill.description: Enforces a waterfall development workflow with requirements, design, implementation, testing, and completion gates.
skill.summary: Guide feature work through strict waterfall phase gates backed by a local features.yml validator.
skill.author: Chemiseblanc

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk weak-cryptography patterns, but review found these are false positives from workflow prose, gate labels, and ordinary validation logic. The skill does instruct the agent to run a hardcoded local validator script that reads features.yml, so the residual risk is low and publishable with disclosure.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded Local Validator Execution
low_findings.description: The skill tells the agent to run ./scripts/validate-gates.py. This is an external command, but the command is hardcoded and the script only validates local feature metadata.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The execution instruction is explicit and points to an included script. Review found no dynamic command construction or shell argument injection.

low_findings.title: Local features.yml Read
low_findings.description: The validator reads features.yml from the current project. This is expected for gate validation, but it is still filesystem access that should be disclosed.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file access is direct and limited to features.yml through pathlib and open. No evidence found of broader filesystem traversal or secret collection.

low_findings.title: False Positive Weak Cryptography Alerts
low_findings.description: The high-severity weak-cryptography detections are false positives. The cited lines contain markdown prose, phase names, requirement checks, or validation branches, not cryptographic algorithms.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found no hashing, encryption, cipher selection, or security-sensitive cryptographic code. The flagged text is documentation and workflow terminology.

low_findings.title: False Positive Markdown Backtick Alerts
low_findings.description: Most Ruby or shell backtick detections are markdown inline-code examples for file paths and blueprint format, not executable code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited backticks are inside markdown tables, code fences, and inline references. They do not execute and do not form a shell command.

low_findings.title: False Positive System Reconnaissance Alerts
low_findings.description: The system reconnaissance detections are false positives from iterating over test identifiers in feature metadata. The code does not inspect host, user, process, or network state.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced loop checks whether test IDs exist in a local test-cases map. No system calls or reconnaissance APIs are present.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Command Invocation Instruction
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill workflow instructs the agent to invoke an included validator script. The target is fixed, but marketplace users should know the skill can cause command execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command is clearly documented and local. Risk is limited because no user-controlled command string is constructed.

dangerous_patterns.title: Local YAML File Parsing
dangerous_patterns.description: The validator parses features.yml with yaml.safe_load_all. This is necessary for the skill and uses the safe loader, but it processes project-controlled YAML.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The parser call is direct and uses yaml.safe_load_all, which avoids arbitrary object construction. The remaining risk is malformed or large local input.

content.user_title: Manage Feature Work With Phase Gates
content.value_statement: Feature work can drift when requirements, design, implementation, and testing happen out of order. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code a strict waterfall checklist with local gate validation.
actual_capabilities: Activates when a project has features.yml or the user requests waterfall workflow.
Runs a local gate validator before feature work continues.
Blocks phase transitions when required feature metadata is missing.
Defines five phases from requirements through completion.
Provides blueprint format guidance for implementation planning.
Checks completion criteria for requirements, linked tests, and passing test status.
limitations: Requires a compatible features.yml structure in the project root.
Depends on the separate feature-file skill when creating missing feature metadata.
Does not execute project tests; it checks recorded test status in features.yml.
Uses a local Python script and pyyaml dependency for validation.

use_cases.title: Control Enterprise Feature Delivery
use_cases.description: Apply explicit phase gates before design, implementation, testing, and completion work proceeds.

use_cases.title: Keep Agents Aligned With Requirements
use_cases.description: Prevent Claude, Codex, or Claude Code from coding before requirements and design records are ready.

use_cases.title: Audit Completion Evidence
use_cases.description: Check that requirements are complete, linked to tests, and marked against passing test cases.

prompt_templates.title: Start Waterfall Work
prompt_templates.scenario: Begin a feature with the workflow enabled.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the waterfall-development workflow for this feature. Validate the current gates and tell me which phase can proceed.

prompt_templates.title: Review Phase Readiness
prompt_templates.scenario: Check whether a feature can move to the next phase.
prompt_templates.prompt: Validate the feature gates for the target phase Design and list only the blockers I must fix.

prompt_templates.title: Plan Implementation
prompt_templates.scenario: Create a structured implementation blueprint after design is ready.
prompt_templates.prompt: Using the waterfall blueprint format, prepare an implementation plan for the approved requirements and design decisions.

prompt_templates.title: Verify Completion
prompt_templates.scenario: Confirm that a feature is ready to close.
prompt_templates.prompt: Run the waterfall completion checks for this feature. Verify requirements, tested-by links, referenced test cases, and passing status.

output_examples.input: Validate whether the payment retry feature can enter implementation.
output_examples.output: The feature cannot enter implementation. Requirements are present, but the decisions field is missing.

output_examples.input: Create an implementation blueprint for the approved design.
output_examples.output: The response outlines approach, key files, ordered steps, security considerations, dependencies, risks, and testing recommendations.

output_examples.input: Check if the feature is complete.
output_examples.output: The feature is not complete until every requirement is Complete, linked tests exist, and all listed tests are passing.

best_practices: Keep features.yml current before asking the agent to change code.
Record design decisions before implementation starts.
Link each completed requirement to at least one test case.
anti_patterns: Skipping gate failures to continue coding faster.
Marking tests as passing before they have been run.
Using the workflow for exploratory prototypes with changing requirements.

faq.question: Does this skill write application code?
faq.answer: It guides when code work may happen. It does not provide a framework-specific implementation by itself.

faq.question: What file does it require?
faq.answer: It expects features.yml in the project root, using the structure checked by the validator.

faq.question: Can it create features.yml?
faq.answer: The skill says it can create features.yml through the separate feature-file skill when invoked.

faq.question: Does it run tests?
faq.answer: No. It checks test metadata and passing status recorded in features.yml.

faq.question: Why is there a security warning?
faq.answer: The workflow runs a local Python validator script and reads features.yml, so command and filesystem access should be disclosed.

faq.question: Is it safe to publish?
faq.answer: Yes. Review found no malicious behavior, prompt injection, network access, or secret collection.
